metadata.title: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
metadata.journal: American Journal of Physiology - Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Experimental study with stable isotope labeled tracers measuring whole body glycerol and glucose kinetics at 14 and 22 hours of fasting
metadata.demographics: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat (24±2% and 23±2% body weight as fat, respectively)

evidence.quote: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
BETTINA MITTENDORFER, JEFFREY F. HOROWITZ, AND SAMUEL KLEIN
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 281: E1333–E1339, 2001
Six women and six men who were matched for adiposity (24±2 and 23±2% body wt as fat, respectively)
Substrate kinetics were measured by infusing stable isotope labeled tracers of glucose ([2H2]glucose) and glycerol ([2H5]glycerol)

methods_participants.label: Participant count and demographics
methods_participants.value: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat

evidence.quote: Six men (32 6 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 6 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participatedinthisstudy

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: All subjects considered to be in good health after comprehensive medical evaluation including history, physical examination, electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

evidence.quote: All subjects were considered to be in good health after completing a comprehensive medical evaluation that included a history and physical examination, an electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - medications and smoking
methods_participants.value: All subjects had stable body weight for ≥2 months and had been sedentary (regular exercise <1 h/wk) for ≥6 months before the study

evidence.quote: None of the subjects was taking regular medications or smoked tobacco

methods_participants.label: Body composition assessment method
methods_participants.value: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR 1000/w) to determine fat mass and fat-free mass

evidence.quote: Each subject's fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR1000/w, Waltham, MA)

methods_participants.label: Pre-study meal timing and composition
methods_participants.value: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body weight (55% carbohydrate, 30% fat, 15% protein)

evidence.quote: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body wt (55% of total energy as carbohydrate, 30% as fat, and 15% as protein)

methods_participants.label: Isotope infusion protocol repetition timing
methods_participants.value: Blood samples obtained before restarting isotope infusions and every 10 minutes between 1730 and 1800 for the 22-hour fasting period

evidence.quote: At 0530, catheters were inserted into a forearm vein for isotope infusion and into a radial artery for blood sampling
Plasma insulin (13) and glucagon (15) concentrations were measured by radioimmunoassay

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc comparisons using Student's t-test for multiple comparisons

evidence.quote: A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with gender and time as factors, was used to test for significant differences in substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting. Significant F-ratios were followed by post hoc comparisons with the use of Student's t-test for multiple comparisons
A value of P #0.05 was considered to be statistically significant
[6,6-2H 2 ]glucose (priming dose: 18 mmol/kg; infusion rate: 0.22 mmolzkg body wt21zmin21; Cambridge Isotope Laboratories, Andover, MA)

narrative_results.statement: Basal glycerol rate of appearance in plasma was greater in women than in men

evidence.quote: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (Ra) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

evidence.quote: These data demonstrate the presence of sexual dimorphism in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass_1 describes catheter insertion timing and procedure; pass_2 describes catecholamine assay method. These are completely different facts from different sections of the paper, indicating misalignment

Pass_1 describes catecholamine measurement using 'single isotope derivative radioenzymatic method'. Pass_2 describes glucose concentration measurement using 'automated glucose analyzer'. These are completely different analytes and methods - not the same fact.

Pass 1 describes plasma glucose measurement using automated glucose analyzer; Pass 2 describes glycerol concentration assay using GC-MS. These are measurements of different analytes using different methods.
Pass 1 describes plasma glycerol measurement using GC-MS; Pass 2 describes fatty acid concentration assay using gas chromatography. Different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes plasma fatty acid measurement using GC; Pass 2 describes glycerol and glucose TTR assay using GC-MS. Different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes plasma tracer-to-tracee ratio measurement using GC-MS; Pass 2 describes fasting duration (14 hours). Completely different measurement types.

Pass 1 focuses on fatty acid and glycerol being higher in women at 14h. Pass 2 discusses increases in fatty acids and ketone bodies with decreases in glucose during fasting, with different emphasis and scope. These describe different aspects and cannot both be the primary finding.
Pass 1 states insulin was lower in women at 22h. Pass 2 states lipolytic rates were similar at 22h. These are different measurements and findings that conflict in their gender comparison conclusions.

semantic_groups.rationale: Both describe identical inclusion criteria for health status with negligible wording differences ('considered to be in' vs 'considered in')
Both extractions are identical regarding medications and smoking inclusion criteria
Same factual content about body weight stability and exercise; only difference is category label ('body weight stability' vs 'body weight and exercise')
Both extractions are identical regarding menstrual cycle phase inclusion criteria
Both describe the same body composition assessment method (DEXA with Hologic QDR 1000/w); pass_1 includes measurement parameters while pass_2 omits them, but core fact is identical
Both extractions contain identical information about pre-study meal timing and composition
Both describe identical pre-study liquid formula details; only difference is category label ('timing and composition' vs just 'liquid formula')

Pass_1 describes catheter insertion timing and procedure; pass_2 describes catecholamine assay method. These are completely different facts from different sections of the paper, indicating misalignment

Both describe the same glucose isotope infusion protocol with identical infusion rate (0.22 mmol/kg body weight/min) and priming dose (18 mmol/kg). Minor wording differences in abbreviation (body wt/min vs body weight/min).
Both describe the same glycerol isotope infusion protocol with identical infusion rate (0.12 mmol/kg body weight/min) and priming dose (1.8 mmol/kg). Minor abbreviation differences.

Pass_1 provides complete blood sampling timing context (14-hour fasting, specific time window 0930-1000). Pass_2 only extracts the sampling frequency (every 10 minutes) without temporal context. Same underlying fact but significantly different completeness.
Pass_1 describes blood sampling timing for 22-hour fasting period (1730-1800). Pass_2 only extracts the fasting duration (22 hours). Related but different aspects of the same measurement period.
Pass_1 contains information about isotope infusion protocol repetition timing at 1430. Pass_2 is empty/null, so this fact is only present in one extraction.
Both describe plasma insulin measurement using radioimmunoassay. Only minor case difference in abbreviation presentation.
Both describe plasma glucagon measurement using radioimmunoassay. Only minor case difference in abbreviation presentation.

Pass_1 describes catecholamine measurement using 'single isotope derivative radioenzymatic method'. Pass_2 describes glucose concentration measurement using 'automated glucose analyzer'. These are completely different analytes and methods - not the same fact.

Pass 1 describes plasma glucose measurement using automated glucose analyzer; Pass 2 describes glycerol concentration assay using GC-MS. These are measurements of different analytes using different methods.
Pass 1 describes plasma glycerol measurement using GC-MS; Pass 2 describes fatty acid concentration assay using gas chromatography. Different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes plasma fatty acid measurement using GC; Pass 2 describes glycerol and glucose TTR assay using GC-MS. Different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes plasma tracer-to-tracee ratio measurement using GC-MS; Pass 2 describes fasting duration (14 hours). Completely different measurement types.
Both describe two-way ANOVA with gender and time as factors followed by post hoc Student's t-test comparisons. Minor wording differences in describing the post hoc procedure.
Both specify P ≤ 0.05 as the significance threshold. Only minor wording difference in label.
Both describe identical participant demographics: six men (32 ± 4 yr) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr) matched on percent body fat. Only minor label variation.
Pass 1 is empty/missing. Pass 2 describes glucose isotope infusion priming dose of 18 mmol/kg using [6,6-2H2]glucose. Only one side present.
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains data about glucose isotope infusion duration (210 minutes).
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains data about glycerol isotope infusion priming dose (1.8 mmol/kg).
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains data about glycerol isotope infusion duration (90 minutes).
Both describe the same finding: glycerol Ra increase is blunted in women vs men during fasting. 'Ra' and 'rate of appearance' are equivalent terminology.
Both state glucose Ra/rate of appearance was similar between genders at 14h and 22h fasting. Minor wording differences (abbreviation vs full term, 'h' vs 'hours').
Identical statements about sexual dimorphism in lipid but not glucose metabolism during basal and short-term fasting.

Pass 1 focuses on fatty acid and glycerol being higher in women at 14h. Pass 2 discusses increases in fatty acids and ketone bodies with decreases in glucose during fasting, with different emphasis and scope. These describe different aspects and cannot both be the primary finding.
Pass 1 states insulin was lower in women at 22h. Pass 2 states lipolytic rates were similar at 22h. These are different measurements and findings that conflict in their gender comparison conclusions.
Pass 1 describes epinephrine increase being greater in men than women between 14-22h fasting. Pass 2 is empty/missing.
Both state basal glycerol rate of appearance was greater in women than men. Minor difference: Pass 1 includes 'gender difference' tag, Pass 2 omits it.
Pass 2 describes findings in female vs male rats regarding fat use and lean body mass preservation. Pass 1 is empty/missing. This appears to be from a different study or section.
